title: The Process of Devotional Service
sanskrit:
man-manā bhava mad-bhakto
mad-yājī māṁ namaskuru
mām evaiṣyasi satyaṁ te
pratijāne priyo 'si me
translation: Engage your mind always in thinking of Me, become My devotee, offer obeisances to Me and worship Me. Being completely absorbed in Me, surely you will come to Me.
summary: Srila Prabhupada explains the essence of the ninth chapter of Bhagavad-gita, emphasizing the absolute necessity of directing the mind towards Krishna. He describes how devotional service is not a sentiment but a practical, active science that satisfies the soul.
transcript:
We must think of Kṛṣṇa. This is the process of devotional service. Kṛṣṇa says, man-manā bhava mad-bhakto mad-yājī māṁ namaskuru. Engage your mind always in thinking of Me, become My devotee, offer obeisances to Me and worship Me. Being completely absorbed in Me, surely you will come to Me. This is the simple instruction given by the Supreme Lord.

Now, how to think of Kṛṣṇa? If we do not know Kṛṣṇa, how can we think of Him? Therefore, we have to hear about Kṛṣṇa. The hearing process is called śravaṇam. Śravaṇaṁ kīrtanaṁ viṣṇoḥ smaraṇam. Hearing is the first step. If you do not hear about Kṛṣṇa, you cannot understand. And if you cannot understand, you cannot think of Him. So, this Krishna Consciousness movement is meant to give you the opportunity to hear about Kṛṣṇa. We are reading Bhagavad-gita, Srimad-Bhagavatam. Through these Vedic scriptures, we can hear about the character, name, qualities, and pastimes of the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

title: Dharma: The Supreme Occupation of the Soul
sanskrit:
sa vai puṁsāṁ paro dharmo
yato bhaktir adhokṣaje
ahaituky apratihatā
yayātmā suprasīdati
translation: The supreme occupation [dharma] for all humanity is that by which men can attain to loving devotional service unto the transcendent Lord. Such devotional service must be unmotivated and uninterrupted to completely satisfy the self.
summary: In this class, Srila Prabhupada defines the true meaning of 'dharma' as the eternal occupation of the spirit soul (sanatana-dharma), rather than sectarian religious beliefs. He highlights that only selfless and uninterrupted service to Krishna can bring complete peace.
transcript:
The Sanskrit word 'dharma' is translated into English as 'religion'. But this is not very adequate. Religion is a kind of faith which can be changed. You can change your faith from Hindu to Christian or Christian to Muslim, but dharma cannot be changed. Dharma means the characteristic which cannot be separated from the object. For example, fire has heat and light. You cannot separate heat and light from fire. Similarly, the spirit soul has an eternal characteristic: rendering service.

Every one of us is rendering service to someone. A father serves his family, a husband serves his wife, a citizen serves the state, and if someone has no one to serve, he keeps a pet dog or cat to serve. So service is our eternal nature. When this service is directed towards the Supreme Lord, it is called bhakti. And it must be ahaitukī, meaning without any material motivation, and apratihatā, uninterrupted by material conditions. If we can develop such loving service, our heart will be completely satisfied, yayātmā suprasīdati.

title: Transmigration of the Soul
sanskrit:
dehino 'smin yathā dehe
kaumāraṁ yauvanaṁ jarā
tathā dehāntara-prāptir
dhīras tatra na muhyati
translation: As the embodied soul continuously passes, in this body, from boyhood to youth to old age, the soul similarly passes into another body at death. A sober person is not bewildered by such a change.
summary: A fundamental lecture explaining the distinction between the temporary body and the eternal spirit soul. Srila Prabhupada utilizes logical, scientific examples of bodily changes to illustrate how the soul transitions to a new body at the time of death.
transcript:
The first lesson of spiritual realization is to know that we are not this body. Kṛṣṇa starts His instructions in the Bhagavad-gita from this point: dehino 'smin yathā dehe kaumāraṁ yauvanaṁ jarā. Dehinaḥ means the owner of this body. The body is changing, but the owner is there. You can understand this easily. You had a child's body; that body is now gone. You had a young man's body; that body is also gone. Now you have an old body. But you know that you are the same person who once had a child's body.

This proves that the self, the soul, remains constant while the material body changes. The ultimate change is called death, when this body stops functioning and the soul enters another body. Tathā dehāntara-prāptiḥ. Dehāntara means another body. A dhīra, a sober person, is not bewildered by this. One who is educated in the science of the soul knows that death is merely a change of dress.

title: Conversations on Mind Control & Chanting
summary: An informal room conversation with disciples and guests where Srila Prabhupada details the easiest way to control the mind in the age of Kali. He compares mind control through silent meditation vs. the chanting of the Holy Names.
transcript:
Guest: Swamiji, my mind wanders constantly during meditation. It seems impossible to control.

Prabhupāda: Yes, because you are trying to make it blank. The mind cannot be blank. The mind's nature is to accept and reject. You must give it positive engagement. If you simply say, 'I will not think of anything,' that is artificial. You cannot remain in that state for more than a few seconds.

Disciple: So how do we control it practically?

Prabhupāda: By chanting Hare Kṛṣṇa. Chanting is the sound vibration of the Supreme. The tongue chants and the ears hear. When you hear the sound vibration 'Kṛṣṇa,' the mind is forced to think of Kṛṣṇa. It has no choice. This is the simple and sublime process. Anyone, even a child, can chant and achieve this meditation immediately.

title: Debunking Scientific Theories of Life
summary: During a morning walk in London, Srila Prabhupada challenges the scientific claim that life originates from chemicals. He raises philosophical arguments, calling on scientists to demonstrate their theories by generating life from a test tube.
transcript:
Disciple: Srila Prabhupada, the modern biologists are claiming that they will very soon create life in the laboratory using chemical compounds.

Prabhupāda: They are saying 'very soon,' 'in the future.' Why not now? If their science is complete, let them make a simple egg or a seed. If you give them chemicals, can they produce an egg that can hatch into a chicken? No. They can only make plastic eggs.

Disciple: They say it takes millions of years of evolution for life to form.

Prabhupāda: That is another bluff. If it takes millions of years, how can they claim they will do it 'soon' in the laboratory? It is a contradiction. The truth is, life comes from life. Kṛṣṇa is the source of all life. Janmādy asya yataḥ. Chemicals are matter, and matter is dead. You cannot produce life from dead matter without the touch of the spirit soul.

title: The Ishavasya Principle: God's Ownership
sanskrit:
īśāvāsyam idaṁ sarvaṁ
yat kiñca jagatyāṁ jagat	tena tyaktena bhuñjīthā
mā gṛdhaḥ kasya svid dhanam
translation: Everything animate or inanimate that is within the universe is controlled and owned by the Lord. One should therefore accept only those things necessary for himself, which are set aside as his quota, and one should not accept other things, knowing well to whom they belong.
summary: Srila Prabhupada delivers a powerful lecture on spiritual economics. He explains that global crises are caused by greedy hoarding, and that peace can be restored if humanity recognizes the supreme ownership of God (Ishavasya).
transcript:
This universe is the property of the Supreme Lord. Everything is owned by Him. Tena tyaktena bhuñjīthā: you can enjoy only what is allotted to you as your quota. Do not encroach upon another's property. If you recognize this simple truth, there will be no economic distress, no war.

Take the example of the birds. If you place a bag of rice in the street, birds will come, eat a few grains, and fly away. They do not take more than their quota. But if you leave a bag of rice there, a human being will immediately take the whole bag and store it in his house, even if he doesn't need it. This greediness is the cause of all problems. We must learn to be satisfied with our quota, knowing that everything belongs to Kṛṣṇa.

title: The Ultimate Question and Answer
sanskrit:
varīyān eṣa te praśnaḥ
kṛto loka-hitaṁ nṛpa
ātmavit-sammataḥ puṁsāṁ
śrotavyādiṣu yaḥ paraḥ
translation: My dear King, your question is glorious because it is very beneficial to all kinds of people. The answer to this question is the prime subject matter for hearing, and it is approved by all transcendentalists.
summary: Delivered in the sacred land of Mayapur, Srila Prabhupada describes the meeting between Sukadeva Gosvami and King Pariksit. He explains that discussing Krishna is the ultimate subject matter of hearing (sravanam) at the time of death.
transcript:
King Parīkṣit was given seven days to live due to a curse. He immediately left his kingdom and went to the bank of the Ganges to prepare for death. He asked the great sages assembled there: 'What is the duty of a man who is about to die?' This is the most important question. Every one of us is about to die. Whether it is in seven days or seventy years, death is certain.

Śukadeva Gosvāmī congratulated the King, saying varīyān eṣa te praśnaḥ: 'Your question is glorious.' Why? Because it concerns the self, ātmavit-sammataḥ. It is approved by all self-realized souls. Most people in the world are busy hearing about politics, newspapers, sports, and business. But at the time of death, none of these topics can save them. Only hearing about the Supreme Lord, Viṣṇu, can deliver the soul from the cycle of birth and death.

title: The Call for Total Surrender
sanskrit:
sarva-dharmān parityajya
mām ekaṁ śaraṇaṁ vraja
aham tvāṁ sarva-pāpebhyo
mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ
translation: Abandon all varieties of religion and just surrender unto Me. I shall deliver you from all sinful reactions. Do not fear.
summary: Srila Prabhupada analyzes the final instruction of the Bhagavad-gita, explaining what it means to abandon all other 'dharmas' and surrender completely to Krishna. He addresses common fears regarding surrender and describes the supreme safety it offers.
transcript:
This is the final conclusion of the Bhagavad-gīta. Kṛṣṇa has explained karma-yoga, jñāna-yoga, dhyāna-yoga, and various other processes. But at the end He says, 'Forget all these. Just surrender to Me.' Sarva-dharmān parityajya. In this material world, we have created so many artificial duties and designations. I think, 'I am a Hindu,' 'I am a Christian,' 'I am an American,' 'I am a leader.' These are all temporary designations.

To surrender to Kṛṣṇa means to give up these false designations and recognize our true, eternal position: we are eternal servants of Kṛṣṇa. Jīvera 'svarūpa' haya-kṛṣṇera 'nitya-dāsa'. People are afraid of surrendering because they think they will become zero. But when you surrender to Kṛṣṇa, you do not become zero; rather, you find your real life and activity. Kṛṣṇa promises, 'Do not fear, I will protect you from all sinful reactions.'
